lessons.title: Oscillation Fundamentals & the Barkhausen Criterion
lessons.content:
An **oscillator** turns DC supply power into a sustained periodic output with *no* AC input. The classic model is an amplifier of gain $A$ wrapped in a frequency-selective feedback network $\beta$, connected as **positive feedback**. The closed-loop gain is

$$\frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}.$$

As the **loop gain** $A\beta \to 1$, the denominator goes to zero: a finite output exists with zero input. That is the **Barkhausen criterion**, two conditions that must hold *simultaneously at one frequency*:

1. **Magnitude:** $|A\beta| = 1$ (the signal returns at full strength).
2. **Phase:** $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ$ (or any multiple of $360^\circ$) — the feedback is regenerative.

The feedback network's frequency-dependent phase is what *selects* the oscillation frequency: only where the total loop phase is $0^\circ$ can the criterion be met.

A subtlety experienced engineers care about: for reliable **startup** you design $|A\beta|$ slightly **greater** than 1, so noise grows. Sustained at exactly 1 would never start from noise. Something must then pull it back to unity once the amplitude is large — otherwise the output grows until it slams into the rails (hard clipping, high distortion). That something is a **nonlinearity or AGC**: a lamp, JFET, diode limiter, or the amplifier's own saturation gently reduces effective gain as amplitude rises, parking the loop at $|A\beta| = 1$. So an oscillator is fundamentally a *conditionally unstable* feedback loop with amplitude regulation bolted on.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Positive feedback loop with forward gain A and feedback network beta summed back to the input; oscillation when loop gain A-beta equals 1.

items.front: Define an electronic oscillator.
items.back: A circuit that produces a sustained, self-starting periodic AC output (sine, square, triangle, etc.) with no AC input, converting DC supply power into a periodic waveform.
items.hint: Output with no input.

items.front: State the two parts of the Barkhausen criterion.
items.back: At the oscillation frequency the loop gain must satisfy: (1) magnitude $|A\beta| = 1$, and (2) phase $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ$ (i.e. an integer multiple of $360^\circ$). Both must hold at the same frequency.
items.hint: One magnitude condition, one phase condition.

items.front: Why design the startup loop gain greater than 1 rather than exactly 1?
items.back: With $|A\beta| > 1$, circuit noise at the target frequency grows exponentially so the oscillator self-starts. At exactly 1 it could only sustain an existing amplitude, never build one from noise.
items.hint: It has to grow from noise.

items.front: What sets the final amplitude of a sinusoidal oscillator?
items.back: A nonlinearity or AGC element (lamp, JFET, diode clamp, or amplifier saturation) that reduces effective gain as amplitude rises, driving the loop back to $|A\beta| = 1$ at a stable amplitude.
items.hint: Gain has to fall as amplitude grows.

items.front: Which Barkhausen condition determines the oscillation frequency?
items.back: The phase condition. Oscillation can only occur at the frequency where the total loop phase shift is $0^\circ$ (or $360^\circ$). The frequency-selective feedback network sets where that happens.
items.hint: Frequency selection is a phase property.

items.front: What happens if loop gain stays well above 1 with no amplitude limiting?
items.back: The output grows until it hard-clips against the supply rails, producing a distorted, harmonic-rich waveform instead of a clean sinusoid. Limiting/AGC is needed for low distortion.
items.hint: Think rails and clipping.

items.prompt: An oscillator's loop gain is $A\beta = 1.2\angle 0^\circ$ at the target frequency at startup. What is the expected behavior?
choices: It cannot oscillate because the magnitude is not exactly 1
Oscillation builds up from noise, then amplitude limiting pulls effective gain to 1
It produces a pure sinusoid at exactly 1.2x the supply voltage
The phase condition is violated so no oscillation occurs
items.explanation: Magnitude > 1 with 0-degree phase satisfies startup: noise grows. A nonlinearity then reduces effective gain to park the loop at |A-beta| = 1 at a steady amplitude.

items.prompt: In the feedback model, oscillation corresponds to which condition on the closed-loop expression $A/(1 - A\beta)$?
choices: Numerator equals zero
Denominator equals zero ($A\beta = 1$)
$A\beta$ becomes negative and large
The loop is broken
items.explanation: When the denominator 1 - A-beta = 0, a finite output exists for zero input - the mathematical signature of oscillation. This is the Barkhausen condition A-beta = 1.

items.prompt: Why is positive (regenerative) feedback required for oscillation, unlike most amplifier design?
choices: It reduces distortion
It returns the signal in phase so it reinforces rather than cancels
It lowers output impedance
It widens bandwidth
items.explanation: Oscillation needs the fed-back signal to reinforce the input (0-degree total loop phase). Negative feedback subtracts and stabilizes; positive feedback at one frequency is what sustains oscillation.

lessons.title: RC Oscillators: Wien-Bridge & Phase-Shift
lessons.content:
At audio and low frequencies, inductors are bulky, so we build the frequency-selective network from **R and C**. Two workhorses dominate.

**Wien-bridge oscillator.** A series RC and a parallel RC form a band-pass network feeding the op-amp's non-inverting input. At the center frequency

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC},$$

the network's phase shift is exactly $0^\circ$ and its attenuation is exactly $\tfrac{1}{3}$. To satisfy $|A\beta| = 1$ the non-inverting amplifier must supply a gain of **+3**, set by $1 + R_f/R_1 = 3$. Because the loop phase is $0^\circ$ here, you use the op-amp non-inverting (no extra inversion). Wien bridges are prized for **low distortion** when paired with a soft amplitude-limiter (the classic HP200A used a light bulb in the gain leg as a thermistor-like AGC).

**Phase-shift oscillator.** An inverting amplifier already gives $180^\circ$; a cascade of three identical RC sections supplies the remaining $180^\circ$ at one frequency. For three sections the oscillation frequency is

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}},$$

and the RC ladder attenuates by $\tfrac{1}{29}$, so the amplifier must provide a gain magnitude of **29**. It is simpler but has higher distortion and worse frequency stability than the Wien bridge.

For your software instincts: both are just *analog band-pass filters* placed inside a positive-feedback loop. The filter's center frequency is the oscillation frequency, and its passband loss dictates the gain you must restore. Frequency accuracy is only as good as your R and C tolerances and their temperature drift — which is exactly why precision work moves to crystals.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Wien-bridge oscillator schematic: op-amp with a series RC and parallel RC feedback network to the non-inverting input and a gain-of-3 resistor network on the inverting input.

items.front: What is the oscillation frequency of a Wien-bridge oscillator?
items.back: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, where R and C are the equal values in the series and parallel RC arms. At $f_0$ the network phase shift is $0^\circ$.
items.hint: Same form as an RC corner frequency.

items.front: What amplifier gain does a Wien bridge require, and why exactly that?
items.back: A gain of +3 (non-inverting). At $f_0$ the Wien RC network attenuates by 1/3, so to make $|A\beta| = 1$ the amplifier must restore a factor of 3: $1 + R_f/R_1 = 3$.
items.hint: The network loses 1/3.

items.front: Why does the Wien bridge use the op-amp non-inverting input?
items.back: Because the Wien network contributes $0^\circ$ phase at $f_0$. To meet the $0^\circ$ total loop-phase condition, the amplifier must also be non-inverting (no added $180^\circ$).
items.hint: Network adds 0 degrees, so amp must too.

items.front: What gain magnitude does a three-section RC phase-shift oscillator need?
items.back: A gain of 29 (with sign giving $180^\circ$ inversion). The three-section RC ladder attenuates by 1/29 at the oscillation frequency, so the inverting amplifier must restore a factor of 29.
items.hint: 1/29 loss to overcome.

items.front: Give the oscillation frequency of a three-section RC phase-shift oscillator.
items.back: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ for three identical RC sections, the frequency at which the ladder produces a net $180^\circ$ phase shift.
items.hint: Note the factor of sqrt(6).

items.front: How was amplitude stabilized in the classic HP200A Wien-bridge oscillator?
items.back: An incandescent lamp in the gain-setting leg: as amplitude rises, lamp current heats the filament, its resistance increases, and the gain drops back toward 3 - a thermal AGC giving very low distortion.
items.hint: A light bulb as a thermistor.

items.prompt: A Wien-bridge oscillator uses $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 16\text{ nF}$. Its oscillation frequency is approximately:
choices: ~1 kHz
~10 kHz
~100 kHz
items.explanation: f0 = 1/(2-pi-RC) = 1/(2-pi x 1e4 x 16e-9) ~ 995 Hz, about 1 kHz.

items.prompt: Compared to a Wien-bridge oscillator, the RC phase-shift oscillator generally has:
choices: Lower distortion and better frequency stability
Higher distortion and poorer frequency stability, but simpler topology
Identical performance but needs an inductor
No need for any amplitude limiting
items.explanation: The phase-shift oscillator is simpler (one inverting amp + RC ladder) but its single high-Q-less network gives higher distortion and weaker frequency stability than the Wien bridge.

items.prompt: Why are RC oscillators preferred over LC oscillators at audio frequencies?
choices: RC networks have higher Q
Inductors for low frequencies are large, lossy, and expensive, while R and C are compact
LC circuits cannot oscillate below 1 MHz
RC oscillators do not need an amplifier
items.explanation: At audio frequencies the required inductors are physically large, heavy, and lossy. RC networks set the frequency compactly, which is why Wien-bridge and phase-shift designs dominate low-frequency sine generation.

lessons.title: LC & Crystal Oscillators for RF and Precision
lessons.content:
At RF the frequency-setting element becomes a resonant **LC tank**, $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, with much higher $Q$ than an RC network, giving cleaner sinusoids and better stability.

**Colpitts** uses a **capacitive** divider ($C_1$, $C_2$) across the inductor for feedback; the effective capacitance is the series combination

$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2},\qquad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L\,C_{eq}}}.$$

**Hartley** uses an **inductive** tap ($L_1$, $L_2$) instead; mnemonic — *Colpitts = Capacitors, Hartley = inductive (Hinductors)*. The **Clapp** adds a small series capacitor in the inductor branch to improve stability by swamping transistor capacitance variation.

**Crystal oscillators** replace the tank with a slice of **quartz**, whose piezoelectric mechanical resonance behaves as an extraordinarily high-$Q$ electrical resonator ($Q \sim 10^4$ to $10^6$, versus $\sim 100$ for LC). The quartz model is a series-resonant motional arm ($L_m, C_m, R_m$) in parallel with package capacitance $C_0$, giving two close resonances:

$$f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_m C_m}} \;(\text{series}),\qquad f_p > f_s \;(\text{parallel}).$$

The steep phase slope near resonance means the Barkhausen phase condition is met in an extremely narrow band, so the frequency barely moves with temperature, supply, or load — the reason crystals (and TCXO/OCXO ovenized variants) are the precision-frequency standard for clocks, MCUs, and radio references. From your radar days: the receiver's stable local oscillator (STALO) is crystal-derived precisely because its short-term stability sets the coherent-processing floor.

items.front: What distinguishes a Colpitts from a Hartley oscillator?
items.back: Colpitts uses a capacitive voltage divider ($C_1$, $C_2$) across the tank for feedback; Hartley uses a tapped inductor ($L_1$, $L_2$). Mnemonic: Colpitts = Capacitors, Hartley = inductors.
items.hint: Cap divider vs. inductor tap.

items.front: Give the Colpitts oscillation frequency including the divider capacitance.
items.back: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L\,C_{eq}}}$ where $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$, the series combination of the two divider capacitors.
items.hint: C1 and C2 in series.

items.front: Why do crystal oscillators have far better frequency stability than LC oscillators?
items.back: Quartz's piezoelectric mechanical resonance gives an enormous Q (10^4 to 10^6 vs. ~100 for LC). The very steep phase-vs-frequency slope means the Barkhausen phase condition is met over an extremely narrow band, so frequency hardly shifts with temperature, supply, or load.
items.hint: It is all about Q and phase slope.

items.front: Describe the equivalent circuit of a quartz crystal.
items.back: A motional series arm $L_m$-$C_m$-$R_m$ (the mechanical resonance) in parallel with the electrode/package shunt capacitance $C_0$. This yields a series-resonant frequency $f_s$ and a slightly higher parallel-resonant frequency $f_p$.
items.hint: Motional RLC plus a shunt cap.

items.front: What does the Clapp oscillator add to the Colpitts, and why?
items.back: A small capacitor in series with the tank inductor. It makes the resonance depend mainly on that small fixed cap, swamping the transistor's variable junction capacitances and improving frequency stability.
items.hint: An extra series C to swamp parasitics.

items.front: What are TCXO and OCXO, and why use them?
items.back: Temperature-Compensated and Oven-Controlled Crystal Oscillators. They actively compensate or thermally stabilize the crystal to hold frequency drift to ppm or ppb levels - used where accuracy/stability over temperature is critical (references, GPS, radar STALO).
items.hint: Compensated vs. oven-held crystals.

items.prompt: A Colpitts oscillator has $L = 1\ \mu\text{H}$, $C_1 = 100\text{ pF}$, $C_2 = 100\text{ pF}$. The oscillation frequency is approximately:
choices: ~7.1 MHz
~16 MHz
~50 MHz

items.prompt: Why is an LC tank preferred over an RC network at RF?
choices: LC tanks are cheaper than resistors
The LC tank's much higher Q gives lower phase noise, better stability, and a cleaner sinusoid
RC networks cannot achieve a 0-degree phase shift
Inductors eliminate the need for an active device
items.explanation: A resonant LC tank has far higher Q than an RC band-pass, sharpening frequency selectivity. That yields a purer sine, better frequency stability, and lower phase noise at RF.

items.prompt: A quartz crystal exhibits two closely spaced resonances. They are:
choices: Two series resonances at the same frequency
A lower series resonance $f_s$ and a slightly higher parallel resonance $f_p$
A fundamental and a 10x overtone
Two parallel resonances set by $C_0$ alone
items.explanation: The motional arm sets a series resonance f_s; combined with the shunt C0 it produces a parallel (anti-)resonance f_p just above f_s. Circuits are designed to operate at one or the other.

items.prompt: In a coherent radar receiver, why is a crystal-derived stable local oscillator (STALO) important?
choices: It increases transmit power
Its short-term stability and low phase noise set the floor for coherent/Doppler processing
It removes the need for an antenna
It widens the receiver bandwidth
items.explanation: Coherent processing relies on a phase-stable reference. STALO phase noise and short-term stability directly limit achievable Doppler resolution and clutter cancellation, so a high-stability crystal source is essential.

lessons.title: Relaxation, 555, VCO/PLL & Phase Noise
lessons.content:
Not every oscillator is sinusoidal. **Relaxation oscillators** generate square/triangle waves by repeatedly charging a capacitor through R toward a threshold, then resetting — they exploit RC time constants and a comparator with hysteresis rather than the Barkhausen sinusoidal loop. An op-amp astable charges C between two comparator trip points; the period scales as $T \propto RC$.

The **555 timer** astable is the canonical jellybean version:

$$f \approx \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}.$$

The cap charges through $R_A + R_B$ and discharges through $R_B$, giving an asymmetric duty cycle.

A **voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO)** makes frequency a function of a control voltage, usually by tuning a tank with a **varactor** (a reverse-biased diode whose junction capacitance varies with voltage). The VCO is the engine of the **phase-locked loop (PLL)**: a **phase/frequency detector** compares the VCO (divided by $N$) against a reference, a **loop filter** integrates the error, and that voltage steers the VCO until phase-locked. Then

$$f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref},$$

the basis of **frequency synthesis** — one crystal reference generating a grid of programmable, stable frequencies for every radio and SoC clock.

**Phase noise** is the spectral purity of the oscillator: short-term random phase fluctuations spread power into skirts around the carrier, specified in dBc/Hz at an offset (e.g. $-110$ dBc/Hz at 10 kHz). This is *the* radar-relevant metric: LO phase noise reciprocally mixes strong clutter onto weak targets and sets the coherent dynamic range. A PLL inherits the reference's low close-in phase noise while the VCO dominates far-out — the loop bandwidth trades the two.

items.front: How does a relaxation oscillator differ from a sinusoidal (Barkhausen) oscillator?
items.back: A relaxation oscillator charges/discharges a capacitor through R toward comparator thresholds (with hysteresis), producing square/triangle waves. It relies on RC timing and switching, not on a 0-degree-phase resonant feedback loop generating a sinusoid.
items.hint: Charge to a threshold, then reset.

items.front: Give the 555 astable frequency formula.
items.back: $f \approx \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$. The capacitor charges through $R_A + R_B$ and discharges through $R_B$, so the duty cycle is asymmetric (>50%).
items.hint: 1.44 over (RA + 2RB)C.

items.front: What is a varactor and how does it tune a VCO?
items.back: A varactor is a reverse-biased diode whose junction capacitance decreases as reverse voltage increases. Placing it in an LC tank lets the control voltage change C, and thus the oscillation frequency.
items.hint: Voltage-variable junction capacitance.

items.front: Name the three core blocks of a PLL and their roles.
items.back: Phase/frequency detector (compares reference and divided VCO phase), loop filter (integrates the error into a clean control voltage), and VCO (output whose frequency is steered by that voltage). A divide-by-N in the feedback sets the multiplication.
items.hint: Compare, filter, oscillate.

items.front: In a locked PLL with a divide-by-N feedback, what is the output frequency?
items.back: $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$. Locking forces the divided VCO frequency to equal the reference, so the VCO runs at N times the reference - the basis of frequency synthesis.
items.hint: Multiply the reference by N.

items.front: What is oscillator phase noise and how is it specified?
items.back: Random short-term phase fluctuations that spread carrier power into noise skirts. Specified as single-sideband power relative to the carrier per Hz at a given offset frequency, in dBc/Hz (e.g. -110 dBc/Hz at 10 kHz offset).
items.hint: dBc/Hz at an offset from the carrier.

items.front: Why does LO phase noise limit coherent radar performance?
items.back: Through reciprocal mixing: phase-noise skirts of the local oscillator translate strong clutter returns onto the frequency of weak targets, raising the effective noise floor and limiting Doppler/clutter-cancellation dynamic range.
items.hint: Reciprocal mixing of clutter onto targets.

items.prompt: A 555 astable uses $R_A = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 100\text{ nF}$. The frequency is approximately:
choices: ~2.4 kHz
~4.8 kHz

items.prompt: How does a PLL combine the phase-noise strengths of its reference and VCO?
choices: The VCO sets noise at all offsets
Inside the loop bandwidth the loop tracks the (low-noise) reference; outside it the free-running VCO dominates
The reference dominates at all offsets
Phase noise is eliminated entirely
items.explanation: Within the loop bandwidth the PLL forces the VCO to follow the clean reference (good close-in noise); beyond it the loop can't correct, so the VCO's own noise dominates. Loop bandwidth trades the two.

items.prompt: What primarily makes a 555 astable's duty cycle exceed 50%?
choices: The supply voltage is too high
Charging occurs through $R_A + R_B$ but discharging only through $R_B$
The comparator thresholds are asymmetric by design fault
The output transistor is too slow
items.explanation: The cap charges through RA + RB (longer high time) and discharges only through RB (shorter low time), so the high interval exceeds the low interval, pushing duty cycle above 50%.
